name: The Kid Who Took Over Twitter

video.title: The Kid Who Took Over Twitter
video.global_style_suffix: 3d mannequin documentary, stylized 3d reconstruction environment, dark low-key lighting, deep shadows, one red rim-light accent, desaturated cinematic documentary grade, filmic grain
video.global_negative_prompt: real human face, facial features, eyes, nose, mouth, hair, human skin, photorealistic person, portrait, cartoon, anime, illustration, oversaturated, neon colors, bright cheerful lighting, low quality, deformed

direction_card.hook_style: open mid-moment — someone is already doing something at a specific time of night
direction_card.ending: freeze on the question the story never answered

visual_plan.spine: A hooded figure in a blue bedroom talks the world's most powerful accounts out of their own hands with a telephone. The story is told through the RECORD it left: the charging release, the platform's own confession, the archived post, the licence he once uploaded to prove he was himself. Reconstruction fills only what no document can show. The room is the argument; the paperwork is the proof.

acts.name: The takeover
acts.function: show the impossible thing, then prove it happened
acts.visual_shift: evidence + real faces of the hijacked, fastest cutting

acts.name: The method
acts.function: the weapon was a phone call
acts.visual_shift: reconstruction lives HERE; documents alongside; the pace slows

acts.name: The trail
acts.visual_shift: documents dominate; the hunt is paperwork

acts.name: The close
acts.visual_shift: context + evidence, never literal; ends on the empty chair

cast.name: Clark
cast.look: a faceless matte grey mannequin figure in a grey hoodie with the hood up and dark jeans, smooth blank head, no face, no facial features

cast.name: The employee
cast.look: a faceless matte grey mannequin figure in a light blue shirt and dark trousers with a lanyard badge, smooth blank head, no face

world.description: the dim blue bedroom, closed blinds, three monitors with red rim lights, energy drink cans and tangled cables
night, glass walls, cold blue monitors

motifs.meaning: the actual weapon: a person picking up, wanting to help
his absence, and the flaw that remains

callbacks.what: a hand lifts a ringing desk phone to begin the attack; at the end another hand reaches for a ringing phone, wanting to be helpful
he is hunched in the chair; the video ends on that chair empty, the monitor still glowing
the real faces of the hijacked, then the silenced platform itself

visual_plan.scale_plan: Act 1 cuts fastest and leans on documents and real photographs. Act 2 slows and is where every reconstruction lives, alternating medium reenactment with macro detail. Act 3 is almost entirely documents, held longer. Act 4 returns to archival context and closes on the bedroom, empty.

voice.speaker: Aiden
voice.instruct: Calm, precise male documentary narrator, mid-low register, measured newsreader trust. Present tense storytelling with real pauses: a beat after each reveal, a longer beat before chapter turns. Slightly tighter pace during escalation, settling quiet and thoughtful at the end with falling cadence. Dry humor read completely straight. Quoted dialogue gets a subtle cadence shift, never a character voice.

scenes.camera: slow lateral pan across, steady tracking

scenes.narration: Some of those accounts were tied to an email address.

scenes.narration: The email was tied to a driver's license he had once uploaded to verify himself on a crypto exchange.

scenes.narration: The most careful social engineer in the story had verified his own identity, in writing, to a company that keeps records.

scenes.narration: On the 31st of July, sixteen days after the attack, police arrest him in Tampa.
scenes.image_prompt: [EVIDENCE] DOJ: Three Individuals Charged

scenes.narration: He is seventeen. Florida charges him as an adult, with thirty felony counts.
scenes.image_prompt: [ARCHIVAL] HILLSBOROUGH COUNTY COURTHOUSE

scenes.narration: In March 2021, he takes a plea deal. Three years in prison, three years of probation, under Florida's youthful offender law.
scenes.image_prompt: [EVIDENCE] Hillsborough State Attorney
scenes.camera: slow aerial push-in toward, cinematic drone shot

scenes.narration: Two others were charged in the United States. We are not going to pretend the full picture is settled. Parts of it are still disputed.
scenes.image_prompt: [EVIDENCE] DOJ: Three Individuals Charged

scenes.narration: Twitter changed its internal tools afterwards. It says access is tighter now.
scenes.image_prompt: [EVIDENCE] Twitter: An update on our security incident

scenes.narration: But the method he used is not a software flaw. It is a person, picking up a phone, wanting to be helpful.

research.summary: On 15 July 2020, 17-year-old Graham Ivan Clark and two others phone-phished Twitter employees, seized the internal admin tool, and hijacked about 130 high-profile accounts to run a bitcoin doubling scam. Twitter silenced verified accounts for hours. Clark was arrested 31 July 2020 and pleaded guilty in March 2021.
facts: Attack ran 15 July 2020, roughly 20:00-22:00 UTC; Musk's account posted at 20:17 UTC.
About 130 accounts targeted; 45 actually used to post the scam.
Scam wallets took roughly 110,000 to 118,000 USD in bitcoin.
Coinbase blocked over 1,000 transactions worth about 280,000 USD.
Twitter acknowledged the breach by 21:45 UTC and disabled tweeting for verified accounts.
Method was phone spear-phishing: LinkedIn scraping, fake VPN portal, 2FA codes read aloud.
Graham Ivan Clark, 17, of Hillsborough County, Florida, arrested 31 July 2020; 30 felony counts.
In 2019, aged 16, Clark stole 164 bitcoin from a Seattle investor via a SIM swap.
March 2021 plea: three years prison plus three years probation (Youthful Offender Act).
Co-defendants Mason Sheppard (19) and Nima Fazeli (22) were charged federally.
sources: U.S. Department of Justice charging announcement, 31 July 2020
Evidence: DOJ: Three Individuals Charged — https://www.justice.gov/usao-ndca/pr/three-individuals-charged-alleged-roles-twitter-hack (public domain (US government work))
Evidence: The 2020 Twitter account hijacking — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_Twitter_account_hijacking (screenshot of a public page, used as documentary evidence)
Evidence: Graham Ivan Clark — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graham_Ivan_Clark (screenshot of a public page, used as documentary evidence)
Evidence: Twitter: An update on our security incident — https://blog.x.com/en_us/topics/company/2020/an-update-on-our-security-incident (screenshot of a public page, used as documentary evidence)
Evidence: The scam tweet, archived — https://web.archive.org/web/20200715203000*/twitter.com/elonmusk (screenshot of a public page, used as documentary evidence)
Evidence: FBI San Francisco statement — https://www.fbi.gov/contact-us/field-offices/sanfrancisco/news/press-releases (public domain (US government work))
Evidence: Hillsborough State Attorney — https://www.sao13th.com/ (screenshot of a public page, used as documentary evidence)
